sourceRefs.fallbackText: Go 1.21 includes a preview of a change to for loop scoping that we plan to ship in Go 1.22, removing one of the most common Go mistakes.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Go 1.21 includes a preview of a change to for loop scoping that we plan to ship in Go 1.22, removing one of the most common Go mistakes.

highSignalBlocks.text: Go 1.21 includes a preview of a change to for loop scoping that we plan to ship in Go 1.22, removing one of the most common Go mistakes.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you’ve written any amount of Go code, you’ve probably made the mistake of keeping a reference to a loop variable past the end of its iteration, at which point it takes on a new value that you didn’t want. For example, consider this program:
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you’ve written any amount of Go code, you’ve probably made the mistake of keeping a reference to a loop variable past the end of its iteration, at which point it takes on a new value that you didn’t want. For example,

highSignalBlocks.text: If you’ve written any amount of Go code, you’ve probably made the mistake of keeping a reference to a loop variable past the end of its iteration, at which point it takes on a new value that you didn’t want. For example, consider this program:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: func main() { done := make(chan bool) values := []string{"a", "b", "c"} for _, v := range values { go func() { fmt.Println(v) done <- true }() } // wait for all goroutines to complete before exiting for _ = range values { <-done } }
sourceRefs.textQuote: func main() { done := make(chan bool) values := []string{"a", "b", "c"} for _, v := range values { go func() { fmt.Println(v) done <- true }() } // wait for all goroutines to complete before exiting for _ = range values

highSignalBlocks.text: func main() { done := make(chan bool) values := []string{"a", "b", "c"} for _, v := range values { go func() { fmt.Println(v) done <- true }() } // wait for all goroutines to complete before exiting for _ = range values { <-done } }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The three created goroutines are all printing the same variable v , so they usually print “c”, “c”, “c”, instead of printing “a”, “b”, and “c” in some order.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The three created goroutines are all printing the same variable v , so they usually print “c”, “c”, “c”, instead of printing “a”, “b”, and “c” in some order.

highSignalBlocks.text: The three created goroutines are all printing the same variable v , so they usually print “c”, “c”, “c”, instead of printing “a”, “b”, and “c” in some order.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The Go FAQ entry “What happens with closures running as goroutines?” , gives this example and remarks “Some confusion may arise when using closures with concurrency.”
sourceRefs.textQuote: The Go FAQ entry “What happens with closures running as goroutines?” , gives this example and remarks “Some confusion may arise when using closures with concurrency.”

highSignalBlocks.text: The Go FAQ entry “What happens with closures running as goroutines?” , gives this example and remarks “Some confusion may arise when using closures with concurrency.”

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Although concurrency is often involved, it need not be. This example has the same problem but no goroutines:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Although concurrency is often involved, it need not be. This example has the same problem but no goroutines:

highSignalBlocks.text: Although concurrency is often involved, it need not be. This example has the same problem but no goroutines:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: func main() { var prints []func() for i := 1; i <= 3; i++ { prints = append(prints, func() { fmt.Println(i) }) } for _, print := range prints { print() } }
sourceRefs.textQuote: func main() { var prints []func() for i := 1; i <= 3; i++ { prints = append(prints, func() { fmt.Println(i) }) } for _, print := range prints { print() } }

highSignalBlocks.text: func main() { var prints []func() for i := 1; i <= 3; i++ { prints = append(prints, func() { fmt.Println(i) }) } for _, print := range prints { print() } }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This kind of mistake has caused production problems at many companies, including a publicly documented issue at Lets Encrypt . In that instance, the accidental capture of the loop variable was spread across multiple functions and much more difficult to notice:
sourceRefs.textQuote: This kind of mistake has caused production problems at many companies, including a publicly documented issue at Lets Encrypt . In that instance, the accidental capture of the loop variable was spread across multiple func

highSignalBlocks.text: This kind of mistake has caused production problems at many companies, including a publicly documented issue at Lets Encrypt . In that instance, the accidental capture of the loop variable was spread across multiple functions and much more difficult to notice:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: // authz2ModelMapToPB converts a mapping of domain name to authz2Models into a // protobuf authorizations map func authz2ModelMapToPB(m map[string]authz2Model) (*sapb.Authorizations, error) { resp := &sapb.Authorizations{} for k, v := range m { // Make a copy of k because it will be reassigned with each loop. kCopy := k authzPB, err := modelToAuthzPB(&v) if
sourceRefs.textQuote: // authz2ModelMapToPB converts a mapping of domain name to authz2Models into a // protobuf authorizations map func authz2ModelMapToPB(m map[string]authz2Model) (*sapb.Authorizations, error) { resp := &sapb.Authorizations

highSignalBlocks.text: // authz2ModelMapToPB converts a mapping of domain name to authz2Models into a // protobuf authorizations map func authz2ModelMapToPB(m map[string]authz2Model) (*sapb.Authorizations, error) { resp := &sapb.Authorizations{} for k, v := range m { // Make a copy of k because it will be reassigned with each loop. kCopy := k authzPB, err := modelToAuthzPB(&v) if err != nil { return nil, err } resp.Authz = append(resp.Authz, &sapb.Authorizations_MapElement{ Domain: &kCopy, Authz: authzPB, }) } return resp, nil }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The author of this code clearly understood the general problem, because they made a copy of k , but it turns out modelToAuthzPB used pointers to fields in v when constructing its result, so the loop also needed to make a copy of v .
sourceRefs.textQuote: The author of this code clearly understood the general problem, because they made a copy of k , but it turns out modelToAuthzPB used pointers to fields in v when constructing its result, so the loop also needed to make a

highSignalBlocks.text: The author of this code clearly understood the general problem, because they made a copy of k , but it turns out modelToAuthzPB used pointers to fields in v when constructing its result, so the loop also needed to make a copy of v .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Tools have been written to identify these mistakes, but it is hard to analyze whether references to a variable outlive its iteration or not. These tools must choose between false negatives and false positives. The loopclosure analyzer used by go vet and gopls opts for false negatives, only reporting when it is sure there is a problem but missing others. Othe
sourceRefs.textQuote: Tools have been written to identify these mistakes, but it is hard to analyze whether references to a variable outlive its iteration or not. These tools must choose between false negatives and false positives. The loopcl

highSignalBlocks.text: Tools have been written to identify these mistakes, but it is hard to analyze whether references to a variable outlive its iteration or not. These tools must choose between false negatives and false positives. The loopclosure analyzer used by go vet and gopls opts for false negatives, only reporting when it is sure there is a problem but missing others. Other checkers opt for false positives, accusing correct code of being incorrect. We ran an analysis of commits adding x := x lines in open-source Go code, expecting to find bug fixes. Instead we found many unnecessary lines being added, suggesting instead that popular checkers have significant false positive rates, but developers add the lines anyway to keep the checkers happy.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: One pair of examples we found was particularly illuminating:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: One of these two diffs is a bug fix; the other is an unnecessary change. You can’t tell which is which unless you know more about the types and functions involved.
sourceRefs.textQuote: One of these two diffs is a bug fix; the other is an unnecessary change. You can’t tell which is which unless you know more about the types and functions involved.

highSignalBlocks.text: One of these two diffs is a bug fix; the other is an unnecessary change. You can’t tell which is which unless you know more about the types and functions involved.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For Go 1.22, we plan to change for loops to make these variables have per-iteration scope instead of per-loop scope. This change will fix the examples above, so that they are no longer buggy Go programs; it will end the production problems caused by such mistakes; and it will remove the need for imprecise tools that prompt users to make unnecessary changes t
sourceRefs.textQuote: For Go 1.22, we plan to change for loops to make these variables have per-iteration scope instead of per-loop scope. This change will fix the examples above, so that they are no longer buggy Go programs; it will end the

highSignalBlocks.text: For Go 1.22, we plan to change for loops to make these variables have per-iteration scope instead of per-loop scope. This change will fix the examples above, so that they are no longer buggy Go programs; it will end the production problems caused by such mistakes; and it will remove the need for imprecise tools that prompt users to make unnecessary changes to their code.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To ensure backwards compatibility with existing code, the new semantics will only apply in packages contained in modules that declare go 1.22 or later in their go.mod files. This per-module decision provides developer control of a gradual update to the new semantics throughout a codebase. It is also possible to use //go:build lines to control the decision on
sourceRefs.textQuote: To ensure backwards compatibility with existing code, the new semantics will only apply in packages contained in modules that declare go 1.22 or later in their go.mod files. This per-module decision provides developer co

highSignalBlocks.text: To ensure backwards compatibility with existing code, the new semantics will only apply in packages contained in modules that declare go 1.22 or later in their go.mod files. This per-module decision provides developer control of a gradual update to the new semantics throughout a codebase. It is also possible to use //go:build lines to control the decision on a per-file basis.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Old code will continue to mean exactly what it means today: the fix only applies to new or updated code. This will give developers control over when the semantics change in a particular package. As a consequence of our forward compatibility work , Go 1.21 will not attempt to compile code that declares go 1.22 or later. We included a special case with the sam
sourceRefs.textQuote: Old code will continue to mean exactly what it means today: the fix only applies to new or updated code. This will give developers control over when the semantics change in a particular package. As a consequence of our f

highSignalBlocks.text: Old code will continue to mean exactly what it means today: the fix only applies to new or updated code. This will give developers control over when the semantics change in a particular package. As a consequence of our forward compatibility work , Go 1.21 will not attempt to compile code that declares go 1.22 or later. We included a special case with the same effect in the point releases Go 1.20.8 and Go 1.19.13, so when Go 1.22 is released, code written depending on the new semantics will never be compiled with the old semantics, unless people are using very old, unsupported Go versions .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Go 1.21 includes a preview of the scoping change. If you compile your code with GOEXPERIMENT=loopvar set in your environment, then the new semantics are applied to all loops (ignoring the go.mod go lines). For example, to check whether your tests still pass with the new loop semantics applied to your package and all your dependencies:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Go 1.21 includes a preview of the scoping change. If you compile your code with GOEXPERIMENT=loopvar set in your environment, then the new semantics are applied to all loops (ignoring the go.mod go lines). For example, t

highSignalBlocks.text: Go 1.21 includes a preview of the scoping change. If you compile your code with GOEXPERIMENT=loopvar set in your environment, then the new semantics are applied to all loops (ignoring the go.mod go lines). For example, to check whether your tests still pass with the new loop semantics applied to your package and all your dependencies:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: GOEXPERIMENT=loopvar go test
sourceRefs.textQuote: GOEXPERIMENT=loopvar go test

highSignalBlocks.text: GOEXPERIMENT=loopvar go test

sourceRefs.fallbackText: We patched our internal Go toolchain at Google to force this mode during all builds at the start of May 2023, and in the past four months we have had zero reports of any problems in production code.
sourceRefs.textQuote: We patched our internal Go toolchain at Google to force this mode during all builds at the start of May 2023, and in the past four months we have had zero reports of any problems in production code.

highSignalBlocks.text: We patched our internal Go toolchain at Google to force this mode during all builds at the start of May 2023, and in the past four months we have had zero reports of any problems in production code.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Although we’ve had no production problems, to prepare for that switch, we did have to correct many buggy tests that were not testing what they thought they were, like this:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Although we’ve had no production problems, to prepare for that switch, we did have to correct many buggy tests that were not testing what they thought they were, like this:

highSignalBlocks.text: Although we’ve had no production problems, to prepare for that switch, we did have to correct many buggy tests that were not testing what they thought they were, like this:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: func TestAllEvenBuggy(t *testing.T) { testCases := []int{1, 2, 4, 6} for _, v := range testCases { t.Run("sub", func(t *testing.T) { t.Parallel() if v&1 != 0 { t.Fatal("odd v", v) } }) } }
sourceRefs.textQuote: func TestAllEvenBuggy(t *testing.T) { testCases := []int{1, 2, 4, 6} for _, v := range testCases { t.Run("sub", func(t *testing.T) { t.Parallel() if v&1 != 0 { t.Fatal("odd v", v) } }) } }

highSignalBlocks.text: func TestAllEvenBuggy(t *testing.T) { testCases := []int{1, 2, 4, 6} for _, v := range testCases { t.Run("sub", func(t *testing.T) { t.Parallel() if v&1 != 0 { t.Fatal("odd v", v) } }) } }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In Go 1.21, this test passes because t.Parallel blocks each subtest until the entire loop has finished and then runs all the subtests in parallel. When the loop has finished, v is always 6, so the subtests all check that 6 is even, so the test passes. Of course, this test really should fail, because 1 is not even. Fixing for loops exposes this kind of buggy
sourceRefs.textQuote: In Go 1.21, this test passes because t.Parallel blocks each subtest until the entire loop has finished and then runs all the subtests in parallel. When the loop has finished, v is always 6, so the subtests all check that

highSignalBlocks.text: In Go 1.21, this test passes because t.Parallel blocks each subtest until the entire loop has finished and then runs all the subtests in parallel. When the loop has finished, v is always 6, so the subtests all check that 6 is even, so the test passes. Of course, this test really should fail, because 1 is not even. Fixing for loops exposes this kind of buggy test.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To help prepare for this kind of discovery, we improved the precision of the loopclosure analyzer in Go 1.21 so that it can identify and report this problem. You can see the report in this program on the Go playground. If go vet is reporting this kind of problem in your own tests, fixing them will prepare you better for Go 1.22.
sourceRefs.textQuote: To help prepare for this kind of discovery, we improved the precision of the loopclosure analyzer in Go 1.21 so that it can identify and report this problem. You can see the report in this program on the Go playground. I

highSignalBlocks.text: To help prepare for this kind of discovery, we improved the precision of the loopclosure analyzer in Go 1.21 so that it can identify and report this problem. You can see the report in this program on the Go playground. If go vet is reporting this kind of problem in your own tests, fixing them will prepare you better for Go 1.22.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you run into other problems, the FAQ has links to examples and details about using a tool we’ve written to identify which specific loop is causing a test failure when the new semantics are applied.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you run into other problems, the FAQ has links to examples and details about using a tool we’ve written to identify which specific loop is causing a test failure when the new semantics are applied.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Go 1.21 includes a preview of a change to for loop scoping that we plan to ship in Go 1.22, removing one of the most common Go mistakes.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Go 1.21 includes a preview of a change to for loop scoping that we plan to ship in Go 1.22, removing one of the most common Go mistakes.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you’ve written any amount of Go code, you’ve probably made the mistake of keeping a reference to a loop variable past the end of its iteration, at which point it takes on a new value that you didn’t want. For example, consider this program:
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you’ve written any amount of Go code, you’ve probably made the mistake of keeping a reference to a loop variable past the end of its iteration, at which point it takes on a new value that you didn’t want. For example,

sourceRefs.fallbackText: func main() { done := make(chan bool) values := []string{"a", "b", "c"} for _, v := range values { go func() { fmt.Println(v) done <- true }() } // wait for all goroutines to complete before exiting for _ = range values { <-done } }
sourceRefs.textQuote: func main() { done := make(chan bool) values := []string{"a", "b", "c"} for _, v := range values { go func() { fmt.Println(v) done <- true }() } // wait for all goroutines to complete before exiting for _ = range values

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The three created goroutines are all printing the same variable v , so they usually print “c”, “c”, “c”, instead of printing “a”, “b”, and “c” in some order.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The three created goroutines are all printing the same variable v , so they usually print “c”, “c”, “c”, instead of printing “a”, “b”, and “c” in some order.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The Go FAQ entry “What happens with closures running as goroutines?” , gives this example and remarks “Some confusion may arise when using closures with concurrency.”
sourceRefs.textQuote: The Go FAQ entry “What happens with closures running as goroutines?” , gives this example and remarks “Some confusion may arise when using closures with concurrency.”

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Although concurrency is often involved, it need not be. This example has the same problem but no goroutines:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Although concurrency is often involved, it need not be. This example has the same problem but no goroutines:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: func main() { var prints []func() for i := 1; i <= 3; i++ { prints = append(prints, func() { fmt.Println(i) }) } for _, print := range prints { print() } }
sourceRefs.textQuote: func main() { var prints []func() for i := 1; i <= 3; i++ { prints = append(prints, func() { fmt.Println(i) }) } for _, print := range prints { print() } }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This kind of mistake has caused production problems at many companies, including a publicly documented issue at Lets Encrypt . In that instance, the accidental capture of the loop variable was spread across multiple functions and much more difficult to notice:
sourceRefs.textQuote: This kind of mistake has caused production problems at many companies, including a publicly documented issue at Lets Encrypt . In that instance, the accidental capture of the loop variable was spread across multiple func

sourceRefs.textQuote: // authz2ModelMapToPB converts a mapping of domain name to authz2Models into a // protobuf authorizations map func authz2ModelMapToPB(m map[string]authz2Model) (*sapb.Authorizations, error) { resp := &sapb.Authorizations

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The author of this code clearly understood the general problem, because they made a copy of k , but it turns out modelToAuthzPB used pointers to fields in v when constructing its result, so the loop also needed to make a copy of v .
sourceRefs.textQuote: The author of this code clearly understood the general problem, because they made a copy of k , but it turns out modelToAuthzPB used pointers to fields in v when constructing its result, so the loop also needed to make a

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Tools have been written to identify these mistakes, but it is hard to analyze whether references to a variable outlive its iteration or not. These tools must choose between false negatives and false positives. The loopclosure analyzer used by go vet and gopls opts for false negatives, only reporting when it is sure there is a problem but missing others. Othe
sourceRefs.textQuote: Tools have been written to identify these mistakes, but it is hard to analyze whether references to a variable outlive its iteration or not. These tools must choose between false negatives and false positives. The loopcl

sourceRefs.fallbackText: One pair of examples we found was particularly illuminating:
sourceRefs.textQuote: One pair of examples we found was particularly illuminating:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This diff was in one program:
sourceRefs.textQuote: This diff was in one program:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: for _, informer := range c.informerMap { + informer := informer go informer.Run(stopCh) }
sourceRefs.textQuote: for _, informer := range c.informerMap { + informer := informer go informer.Run(stopCh) }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: And this diff was in another program:
sourceRefs.textQuote: And this diff was in another program:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: for _, a := range alarms { + a := a go a.Monitor(b) }
sourceRefs.textQuote: for _, a := range alarms { + a := a go a.Monitor(b) }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: One of these two diffs is a bug fix; the other is an unnecessary change. You can’t tell which is which unless you know more about the types and functions involved.
sourceRefs.textQuote: One of these two diffs is a bug fix; the other is an unnecessary change. You can’t tell which is which unless you know more about the types and functions involved.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For Go 1.22, we plan to change for loops to make these variables have per-iteration scope instead of per-loop scope. This change will fix the examples above, so that they are no longer buggy Go programs; it will end the production problems caused by such mistakes; and it will remove the need for imprecise tools that prompt users to make unnecessary changes t
sourceRefs.textQuote: For Go 1.22, we plan to change for loops to make these variables have per-iteration scope instead of per-loop scope. This change will fix the examples above, so that they are no longer buggy Go programs; it will end the

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To ensure backwards compatibility with existing code, the new semantics will only apply in packages contained in modules that declare go 1.22 or later in their go.mod files. This per-module decision provides developer control of a gradual update to the new semantics throughout a codebase. It is also possible to use //go:build lines to control the decision on
sourceRefs.textQuote: To ensure backwards compatibility with existing code, the new semantics will only apply in packages contained in modules that declare go 1.22 or later in their go.mod files. This per-module decision provides developer co

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Old code will continue to mean exactly what it means today: the fix only applies to new or updated code. This will give developers control over when the semantics change in a particular package. As a consequence of our forward compatibility work , Go 1.21 will not attempt to compile code that declares go 1.22 or later. We included a special case with the sam
sourceRefs.textQuote: Old code will continue to mean exactly what it means today: the fix only applies to new or updated code. This will give developers control over when the semantics change in a particular package. As a consequence of our f

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Go 1.21 includes a preview of the scoping change. If you compile your code with GOEXPERIMENT=loopvar set in your environment, then the new semantics are applied to all loops (ignoring the go.mod go lines). For example, to check whether your tests still pass with the new loop semantics applied to your package and all your dependencies:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Go 1.21 includes a preview of the scoping change. If you compile your code with GOEXPERIMENT=loopvar set in your environment, then the new semantics are applied to all loops (ignoring the go.mod go lines). For example, t

sourceRefs.fallbackText: GOEXPERIMENT=loopvar go test
sourceRefs.textQuote: GOEXPERIMENT=loopvar go test

sourceRefs.fallbackText: We patched our internal Go toolchain at Google to force this mode during all builds at the start of May 2023, and in the past four months we have had zero reports of any problems in production code.
sourceRefs.textQuote: We patched our internal Go toolchain at Google to force this mode during all builds at the start of May 2023, and in the past four months we have had zero reports of any problems in production code.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: You can also try test programs to better understand the semantics on the Go playground by including a // GOEXPERIMENT=loopvar comment at the top of the program, like in this program . (This comment only applies in the Go playground.)
sourceRefs.textQuote: You can also try test programs to better understand the semantics on the Go playground by including a // GOEXPERIMENT=loopvar comment at the top of the program, like in this program . (This comment only applies in the Go

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Although we’ve had no production problems, to prepare for that switch, we did have to correct many buggy tests that were not testing what they thought they were, like this:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Although we’ve had no production problems, to prepare for that switch, we did have to correct many buggy tests that were not testing what they thought they were, like this:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: func TestAllEvenBuggy(t *testing.T) { testCases := []int{1, 2, 4, 6} for _, v := range testCases { t.Run("sub", func(t *testing.T) { t.Parallel() if v&1 != 0 { t.Fatal("odd v", v) } }) } }
sourceRefs.textQuote: func TestAllEvenBuggy(t *testing.T) { testCases := []int{1, 2, 4, 6} for _, v := range testCases { t.Run("sub", func(t *testing.T) { t.Parallel() if v&1 != 0 { t.Fatal("odd v", v) } }) } }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In Go 1.21, this test passes because t.Parallel blocks each subtest until the entire loop has finished and then runs all the subtests in parallel. When the loop has finished, v is always 6, so the subtests all check that 6 is even, so the test passes. Of course, this test really should fail, because 1 is not even. Fixing for loops exposes this kind of buggy
sourceRefs.textQuote: In Go 1.21, this test passes because t.Parallel blocks each subtest until the entire loop has finished and then runs all the subtests in parallel. When the loop has finished, v is always 6, so the subtests all check that

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To help prepare for this kind of discovery, we improved the precision of the loopclosure analyzer in Go 1.21 so that it can identify and report this problem. You can see the report in this program on the Go playground. If go vet is reporting this kind of problem in your own tests, fixing them will prepare you better for Go 1.22.
sourceRefs.textQuote: To help prepare for this kind of discovery, we improved the precision of the loopclosure analyzer in Go 1.21 so that it can identify and report this problem. You can see the report in this program on the Go playground. I

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you run into other problems, the FAQ has links to examples and details about using a tool we’ve written to identify which specific loop is causing a test failure when the new semantics are applied.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you run into other problems, the FAQ has links to examples and details about using a tool we’ve written to identify which specific loop is causing a test failure when the new semantics are applied.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For more information about the change, see the design document and the FAQ .
sourceRefs.textQuote: For more information about the change, see the design document and the FAQ .
